summary: O'Neil catalogs five notable consumer scams from 2015, arguing that global supply chain opacity enables widespread fraud. From VW's emissions cheating to fake artisanal chocolate to mislabeled yarn, she demonstrates how companies exploit consumers' inability to verify product claims. The common thread is that authentication came from external testing or allergic reactions, not internal accountability. She questions whether we'll develop better authentication systems or simply see scams proliferate.
key_points: Global supply chains are so opaque that consumers cannot verify claims about how products are made or what they contain
The VW scandal was exposed by external road tests, not whistleblowers, revealing a failure of internal accountability
Marketing and branding (like 'artisanal' or 'bamboo') can successfully mask inferior or fraudulent products
Food mislabeling, including horsemeat substitution, is a recurring problem across years and retailers
Consumer protection often depends on individuals with allergies or expert knowledge detecting fraud accidentally
money_quotes: I blame it on our global supply chain that's entirely opaque and impenetrable to the outsider.
We have no idea how things are made, what they're made with, or how the end results get shipped around the world.
Seriously, anything goes. And that's probably not going to change.
What's amazing to me is that no whistleblower did anything; we learned about it from road tests by an external group.
It just goes to show you how much marketing plays a part in this stuff.
We eat horsemeat all the fucking time.
The fiber community was thrown into a tizzy when this came out; we yarn snobs turn up our noses at nylon.
key_insight: The opacity of modern supply chains makes fraud nearly undetectable, leaving consumers dependent on accidental discoveries rather than systemic accountability.
